item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, the Second Hytoth was to the first Hytoth was SCP-3125 is to our reality? And whatever lock was containing it was unlocked and then… SCP-3125 was released?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder what would happen if the entities from before the pattern would take full and permanent control over the panda's
Those guys are not living to their new potential… Maybe because the aspect allowing them to was left behind when they get here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm upvoting because I get the general IDEA of the article, and I like it, but I understand that not a lot of people DO get it… To be perfectly honest, I'm not sure how to explain.

The general consensus seems to be: Amazing article, too much purple prose, no idea what's going on.

On a side note: you could remove any and all mention of bamboo from this article and it would feel exactly the same. The computer aspects only vaguely come into play, and it's really more about primordial spirits that possess pandas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The purple put me off. I detest purple, ambiguous and seemingly nonsensical rambling that you have to visit the comments to understand. Downvoted

SCP-2528-C-1: I return.

Upvoted at this line. Congrats on triple digits! There are a lot of fascinating concepts here, I like Dr. Gāo's surprise at sudden ominous panda speech the most.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changes reverted- bambusinae is a subclade of bambuseae which is a subclade of bambusoideae which itself a subclade of the BOP clade. In other words, its one of those places where there's more branches than we have formal designations and conventions for, and botany naming conventions are sort of a free-for-all to begin with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … Why is SCP-2528-A referred to as "the clade Bambusoideae bambusinae"? That's not remotely correct by any scientific terminology standards that I could find. Is it supposed to reflect either Foundation-only terminology standards or that scientific terminology would change in the hypothetical future time frame in which this skip's document appears to have been written?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love how, coming out of this article, I feel simultaneously that I understood the story in general, yet am still unsure what the hell I just read.

The part about the talking kung fu pandas was actually the easiest part to decipher.

Beautiful work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I knew these things were not of this reality when the pandas could do things that require appendages that they don't have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A grammatical quibble: the phrase "is comprised of" is incorrect. There are two alternatives:

A is composed of B and C
A comprises B and C

It's a peeve of mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd have to agree. I somewhat enjoyed this, but there was a lot of just confusing rambling that I didn't understand, so no vote for now. And I didn't catch these references to the Pattern Screamers. Apparently there were also references to other articles? I didn't catch those, can someone explain those to me?

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ha me ha me ha!
– hide block

why not take it up to eleven and converse gas to plasma?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After reading the scp then reading all of this, I think something just broke in my brain. I need to go watch some cartoons or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually enjoyed this one a good bit. I didn't mind the purple prose and was able to follow along pretty well. And while a bit silly, the thought of the shrimp panda action is a hard thing to dislike. One question though, the idea of pattern screamers, where had this idea shown up before on the wiki?
I rate this one +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had my interest until the end, but his long speechifying bored me. I'm digging the idea and some of the presentation, but the interviews didn't really interest me and the payoff felt weak. If this same information was presented in a less direct fashion, I think it would be better. No-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For all that sirpudding has found a way it legitimately works, that statement has been overused to show "my character is OP/doesn't give a damn/2edgy4u". Not a reflection upon your article, but one of those pitfalls you can innocently fall into in the course of writing. Also, I may have zoned out a little after reading it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
sirpudding and Deacon_Blues are pretty much correct. The only thing missing is the explanation for why the -Cs wanted to stay hidden. Admittedly, it requires some fridge logic.

If you really want to know
– hide block

They are utterly terrified of us and what we might do. The only path to survival they can see would be to break the Pattern. They don't know what the consequences might be, but anything beats certain extinction.
We, on the other hand, are thriving in the Pattern. For us, the question is "Do you want to potentially sacrifice everything on the possibility of a totally unknown outcome".
Sure, the heat death of the universe might be a bit of an incentive, but they don't think they can hang on long enough for that to become a pressing reason for us to help them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As it stands, it looks like the biggest issues people are having come from the purple prose. In particular, I think the big f-off text wall of the final transcript might be a biiit of a stumbling block. I'm currently experimenting with alternate possibilities.

In the meantime, I thought I'd share the backstory that leads to the particular format with those that are interested.

High Mythic Physics Away!
Well, that's five minutes I'm not getting back.  Why did I click this again?

So, the big difference between pre-Pattern (the High Mythic era) physics and Pattern physics is that High Mythic physics were top down vs reality's bottom up physics model.

In the real world, you have a basic set of principle and actors, and different permutations of those gets you different things at different organizational levels. Different configurations of atoms make up different molecules, different configurations of subatomic particles create different types of atoms, ect. Also, everything that is shares the same model.

The High Mythic worked more along the lines of Platonic Forms, with the caveat that not all Forms are equal. Each Form defines the fundamental essence of what it is to be a particular thing, which finds expression at lower orders of organization. These expressions are referred to as Shadows. It should be noted that Shadows can also be Forms at their order of organization.

Interactions between Forms and Shadows are largely ad hoc affairs, determined by the Forms and Shadows involved. If you have two Shadows of the same Form, they will interact according to the principles of that Form. In other cases1, they're gonna have to figure out how they interact. This is less a process of physics and more a process of politics, and the Shadow that is part of the highest Form involved will almost always get to set the terms.

Example 1
In reality, you walk along the surface of a planet. You can trace that down across organizational layers and you'll find the same actors and principles at work in each. Both you and the planet have protons, neutrons and electrons for example. The principles of the electro-weak that keep you from falling through the earth apply to both you and planet.

In the High Mythic, lets say that you are a Z-Level expression of the Z+1 Form of Humanity, walking across the surface of the Earth, which is ultimately an expression of the Z+2 Form of planet.
Lets say this is the first interaction of Human and Planet at any Z level.
Humanity starts off by contacting the Z+1 Shadows of Planet that Earth is a Shadow of. Human explains the basics of what Humanity is, in particular the concept of "walk". Planet has seen concepts similar to "walk" before, and decides that the precedents allow expressions of Human to exercise "walk" on expressions of Ground.
Human explains that it would really like to exercise "walk" on expressions of Sky as well. Sky chimes in that it has not previously agreed to this, having a strict contract with "fly" and its affiliated concepts. Human does not posses "fly", but wishes to move through Sky so it becomes insistent. It wishes to exercise "walk" with Sky.
Planet informs Human that it is bigger, stronger and has precedent on its side. Human seeks mediation from other Forms at Z+1 and Z+2, but they all agree that Planet is in the right.

Therefore, expressions of Human can "walk" on expressions of Ground, but not Sky. Furthermore, Sea and its subsidiaries chimes in to inform Human that, while in the past it has allowed some Forms to express "walk" with it, Human can piss off for giving Sky so much trouble.

A Form or Shadow's place in the hierarchy is not fixed. Forms that share an organizational level can band together, combining their concepts into a Tribe. Once a Tribe has reached a certain conceptual weight they have a few options. They can bud, creating a new Form at their organizational level, or they can ascend, creating a new higher level Form crystallized around the concepts of the Tribe.
It is important to note that the new Form will be more than the sum of its parts. The Form will begin to express new Shadows of its own. Usually, Forms that were not Shadows of already existing Forms will become Shadows of this new Form.

Example 2
Human (at Z+2 in this example), wishes to traverse Sea. A Z+1 Shadow of Human is the Form Craftsman. Craftsman gets together some Z+1 old friends: the Forms Rowboat, Plank (a budding of Craftsman, Saw and several Z+1 Shadows of Wood), Nail, Hammer and a few others, and forms a Tribe. That Tribe then ascends, propelling Rowboat upwards into the Z+2 Form Ship.
Ship begins to cast Shadows. The first is Rowboat, the Form it used to be. In addition, it begins casting Sailing Ship, Steamship, Ironclad, Kayak, ect.

Ship is grateful to Human, so agrees to negotiate with Sea. Ship gains the right to exercise "sail" with Sea. However, since Sea is still bitter about Human giving Sky trouble, Sea demands that if Ship allows Human the right to "walk" or "ride" on it, then Ship has to accept Sea's right to "sink" Ship. This is problematic, because Human had already agreed to "drown" in exchange for the right to "swim"

One final note is that higher Forms are not beholden to their Shadows. You cannot damage the Form Ship by damaging a particular expression of Ship.

Example 3
In reality, you build a wooden sailing ship. Then you take away all the planks you used to build the ship. Then you try to sail to England2. You are an idiot that jumped into the ocean carrying a bunch of sails and nails and what have you, because the ship no longer exists..

In High Mythic, Plank leaves the Tribe that created Ship. It is still possible for Ship to express Shadows that include concepts from Plank.

The Pattern is an anomaly in this system. It is a basal level Form that extends its Shadows upwards through the organization levels instead of down. Additionally, its conceptual mass is immense, giving it near overwhelming negotiating power (in the same way a gun grants someone negotiating power when deciding of another party lives or dies). Lastly, and most horrifically from the -C perspective, the Pattern can effect higher Forms through their Shadows. This is a bit like being able to knock someone down by kicking their mother's shadow in the legs.

More humorously, the Pattern is the Platonic Ideal of Order standing on its head and kicking the legs out from under all the other Ideals.

Sidenote: All this came from a Tale I was brainstorming based off of SCP-2040, which would have been a memo in the SCP format written by the Foundation outside the Restricted Zone. Containment would have involved splitting off a Shadow that could infiltrate the Zone and destroy it from within. Memo would have a symbol combining aspects of Foundation, GOC and Serpent Hand logos.

Footnotes
1. Two Shadows of different Forms, a Form and the Shadow of the higher Form, two equal Forms
2. Assuming you are not in England. If you are, I don't know, have some tea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They are definitely not the product of artifice, so Artificial Intelligence seems inappropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know if they are AI, I rather suspect they aren't actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think another part of it is that the AIs don't necessarily want to be noticed - being noticed might freak out the others in the world, and that reduces their chance for survival. It's trouble enough just getting the frigging pandas to reproduce, normally not an issue for life-forms; trying to get them to reproduce and avoid danger might overtax the system.

As regards the purple prose: This is an alien mind, one not even vaguely human, trying its level best to communicate concepts that don't really exist in our language. It's bound to wax a bit poetic now and again just trying to say hello, much less express its history and origins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, I can work with that, but still, I'm undecided on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My read:
The Pandas are still Pandas, doing Panda things, but the virtual intelligences that run on the network can use them as peripherals as needed which includes some wuxia stuff because the system can do some reality manipulation. They didn't want to contact us, until the Foundation forced them, so they mostly just let the Pandas be Pandas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eugh. I want to like this. This even seems to use my headcanon on Pattern Screamers. I'm also, like, super surprised that people didn't understand the purple. The purple was a bit obnoxiously repetitive, but that obnoxious repetition helped pull me through it.

But the idea of literally every bamboo shoot and every panda being part of a massive supercomputer just seems, Iunno, off? You'd think that the talking pandas would've come forward before now, and then just ruined the whole Veil thing the Foundation's got going on. Also, the pandas doing other things pandas don't do IRL (like the above-discussed karate/kung-fu/ninjutsu) seems kinda shoehorned and silly.

Why not have a panda take a sharp piece of bamboo and start writing in the dirt? Why does it need to break physics and established laws of biology?

Also, why doesn't this computer network take advantage of other bamboo-eating creatures, like red pandas and probably a ton of invertebrate species?

Neutral vote because, like I said, I want to like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I do precisely this whenever I need to use the name of an IRL species. Like, "Binomial/Scientific name of animal1. This way, if someone doesn't recognize "Canis lupus familiaris," they're brought up to speed by me telling them that it's a dog. Keeps everyone in the loop.

Footnotes
1. Colloquially known as "normal people name of animal."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This week, on Pandas vs Pattern Screamers…

Now, I'm okay with this. It's a cool story and an interesting concept. However, some of the prose is rather a slog to read through and more of something I have to commit to reading than something I really want to read because it's interesting. It's super inaccessible, and yeah, that's always going to be a factor when dealing with complex concepts, but I can't help but think it's not a necessity here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Initially I thought the first research ideas were pretty dumb (Digestion research, ok, but testing if a panda eating bamboo makes it Bixby-proof? And that somehow involving vivisection? That seems less like hypothesis testing and working from first principles and more like something a fourth-grader would suggest. If you have to requisition reality-bending to test something, I would assume you would need a more solid proposal.)

Also, I thought much of the dialog in the initial interviews came off as empty and faux-deep, like badly worded Facebook-repost koans.

That said, I do love me some purple alien dialog about the annihilation and recreation of the universe. That, and the idea that a massively distributed quantum-connected network would be resistant to reality-bending is very clever.

+1

Edit: It's also pretty weird that you link to an actual external page about mantis shrimp by title to explain a redacted box. I think I understand the implications, but it feels very… unscientific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that it appeared to be uttering the melodramatic threat at first, but then quickly clearly was just an alien atheist's dry criticism of the concept of salvation as compared to saving to memory. I found it a pleasant subversion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understood this without spoilers or looking up any binomials (the panda was familiar and bamboo easily inferred from the Latin and the association with pandas). I got that it was a computer (and no, saying it is "computer-like" isn't meaningful, "computer" is what a Turing complete machine does, not what the parts are made of or what changes states to form logic, I was a "computer" in the Corps for a while and I am not electronic) and how it works. I love that it was discovered because of an unrelated parachronic event. Enthusiastically +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1-4) Done.

5) This is the criticism I've been dreading. That bit is another holdover from the more silly incarnation, when C-1 was more of a beleaguered trickster. I know it doesn't belong in this version… but I DON'T WANNA GET RID OF IT! WAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAH!

In all seriousness, do you think an alternate formulation might work better? Most of the first interview is built off of a statement misconstrued as a threat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot, I'm always up for more Pattern Screamers.

That said, it still needs work. A few things I noted:

1) You did it with Phyllostachys edulis, but nothing else: genus name is not capitalized.

2) Watch for close repetition of words: "Whatever passed into that maw was torn asunder, passed through a sieve of razor sharp fractals."

3) You're missing some paragraph breaks.

4) In the final interview, the heart of that one tribe is mentioned as a 'pattern'. Since this is an audio recording, there's no way the transcriptionist would be able to differentiate lowercase and uppercase Pattern, so I'd recommend jettisoning that word.

5) Lastly, starting an interview off with "Your god cannot save you now" is really super cheesy and dumb. Made the middle of this article really shaky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, you gave honest feedback. That's worth as much as an upvote to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's fair if you don't want to budge on it to keep a certain element. God knows I'm guilty of that. However, I can't do anything but downvote because essentially a good chunk of the article reads like someone reading gibberish. And the fact that I got nothing you or Myriad talked about doesn't help it matters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I had no clue what the pattern was until I read the comments. Might have been due to what Decibelles said, the dialogue was pretty dense and loaded, which was rather a slog to read through.

Knowing about the pattern screamers wouldn't have helped you there. They are mentioned in a couple of scifi themed scps, generally as something bad that happened on a galatic scale a long time ago. scp-2173 is one version, but I don't actually like it that much, so it didn't influence this one at all.

As for the rest: I see where you are coming from, but the computer analogy is too integral to this for me to get rid of. I have the image of a researcher examining the results of the most recent round of tests having a eurka moment when they realize that they're looking at a computer system.

Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Sonic PANDA
- *Guile theme plays*

Well here's a nice scholarly article link for pistol shrimp if you decide to go with that instead. http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0077120

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WrongJohnSilver
Hadoken1!

Well… It doesn't really work that way unless the pandas are in the water, because cavitation is necessary - the process by which a pressure differential causes the carrying fluid to change state from liquid to gas, then collapse back to liquid

You are correct! It was a bit of absurdity from an earlier, sillier draft that I could not bear to part with.

Myriad
Hadoken2!

Hrmm, I'm not sure about the science there - I don't think that cavitation is even possible in atmosphere as opposed to liquid.

Also correct!

That said, if you want fireball throwing, you totally want pistol shrimp as opposed to mantis shrimp.

Gah! Still correct, only now it wasn't intentional. Now to decide if I want to change the link or not…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reads to me what "is then eaten in a manner closely resembling the behaviors exhibited by mammals" would to a biologist; it's so strangely non-specific that it comes across as pretty much useless to a professional that actually understands what's being talked about.

Fair enough. You have no idea how much time I spent banging my head against that freaking sentence.
For reference, I was specifically thinking of the architecture of a Coherence Cluster.

Which isn't to say that you should expand on it, maybe the opposite and actually merge it with the sentence before it to read:

So don't do, for example

this?
– hide block

SCP-XXXX-A is comprised of living plants in the clade Bambusoideae Bambusinae. During growth, a shoot of SCP-XXXX-A will anomalously generate particles linked via quantum entanglement throughout its tissues. Several distinct phases of particle generation have been observed.
In the first phase, coinciding with the initial growth of the culm1, pairs will form in the rhizome with half of each pair moving into the culm. These particles take up position in the nodes2 and leaves of the stalk, such that each node and each leaf has at least one connection to the rhizome.
Once the culm has completed growth, entangled particle pairs will begin to form in the areas surrounding rhizome-connecting particles. These particles are organized in such a way as to suggest the structure of a data storage device.
At the same time, the rhizome will begin to generate and share particles with the rhizomes of other instances via anomalous means. This creates a traversable network linking all known instances of SCP-XXXX-A.

SCP-XXXX-B is comprised of the species Ailuropoda Melanoleuca and its attendant digestive bacteria.
The metabolic processes of these organisms induce changes to the SCP-XXXX-A network. As a -B instance digests -A matter, anomalous chemical reactions3 induce changes to the quantum state of the entangled particles being digested4. These changes are detected by the connected -A instances, which propagate them through the network.

Most of the entangled particles consumed by an -B instance will excreted during defecation. However some particles will be absorbed into the -A instance's body, where they will generally migrate to the instance's fatty tissues. These particles are theorized to enable control over -B instances.

The decline in the population of Ailuropoda Melanoleuca has resulted in a parallel decline in the processing power available to SCP-XXXX.

I'll admit, "sapient, data-form intelligences" is sort of a guilty pleasure for me. I am currently fighting with myself over keeping it.

Footnotes
1. Central stalk of a bamboo shoot
2. Solid joints between section, characterized by a swelling encircling the ends of the culm segments
3. It is currently believed that these reactions are mediated by the symbiotic gut bacteria Ailuropoda Melanoleuca relies on for digestion
4. This process has been observed to continue for up to three hours after digested -A matter has been excreted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

The first part for me was

SCP-2528 is a network of organic computers spanning most of East and Southeast Asia.

It does say organic, but it also says it is a computer not computer-like, which is where the confusion start. "System of organisms displaying computer-like characteristics" would work better.

Then there's

SCP-2528 is comprised of two primary component organisms: SCP-2528-A, which acts as data storage and network infrastructure, and SCP-2528-B, which enables data processing and environmental manipulation.

Which leans a bit too much into the computer simile. I'm no biologist, but I think that you could describe this process without having to compare it to a computer that much.

In general, it isn't really a specific section that causes the confusion, but rather small bits here and there that end up being summed. I would say that describing the SCP as a computer once would suffice to bring in the comparison, and the rest should be descriptions of the organisms as an organism without having to constantly go back to the computer analogy.

Knowledge regarding the Pattern Screamers shouldn't be required for this. Might reveal some of the easter eggs, but that's all.

Well I had no clue what the pattern was until I read the comments. Might have been due to what Decibelles said, the dialogue was pretty dense and loaded, which was rather a slog to read through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't understand the logic in thinking they are computers. Their structure may lend themselves to be similar to a network, but does it really mean they are?

Cybernetics, dude- it'll blow your mind. X3

Basically, anything that can receive input, process that input, and respond in some way is a control system: any network connecting enough control systems to achieve some level of recursive feedback is a cybernetic system. Network enough of those together, and you have a computer. There is technically no meaningful difference between "computer" and "network"; its simply a matter of words: whether you mean the box on your desk, or the network of connections that it holds and links to the global network of connections operated by organic networks of connections.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am a ninja shrimp panda master.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple of minor nitpicks from someone who works with computers for a living:

These changes then propagate throughout the SCP-2528-A network in a manner closely resembling the activity seen in a distributed data cluster.

This reads to me what "is then eaten in a manner closely resembling the behaviors exhibited by mammals" would to a biologist; it's so strangely non-specific that it comes across as pretty much useless to a professional that actually understands what's being talked about.

Which isn't to say that you should expand on it, maybe the opposite and actually merge it with the sentence before it to read:

anomalous chemical reactions induce changes to the quantum states of the entangled particles, that connect back to SCP-2528-A instances thus propagating data throughout the entire cluster.

I'm also slightly weirded out by the phrase "sapient, data-form intelligences", as it sounds really technobabble-y to me. Maybe the phrase "sapient entities" would work instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Redacted
- HADOKEN

Hrmm, I'm not sure about the science there - I don't think that cavitation is even possible in atmosphere as opposed to liquid. That said, if you want fireball throwing, you totally want pistol shrimp as opposed to mantis shrimp.

EDIT: I totally got ninjaed by a guy posting about ninja shrimp pandas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
NINJA PANDAS ONLY
Hadouken!

Well… It doesn't really work that way unless the pandas are in the water, because cavitation is necessary - the process by which a pressure differential causes the carrying fluid to change state from liquid to gas, then collapse back to liquid - but hey, I like how it was redacted but still pointed to a footnote about the mantis shrimp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Under the Redacted
– hide block

Fireball throwing mantis-shrimp-style pandas actually. I was thinking of how the mantis shrimp kills with a shockwave of superheated water.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Knowledge regarding the Pattern Screamers shouldn't be required for this. Might reveal some of the easter eggs, but that's all.

I think I might be too close to this to see where the confusion comes from in the technical description. Could point me at the problem areas?

Alternatives to "network of organic computers":
"organic computational matrix"
"system of organisms displaying computer-like characteristics"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, not only that, but imagining ninja-mantis-shrimp-style pandas is absolutely terrific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Normally I like a bit of weirdness or confusion in articles like this, since it tends to give it an extra something that sticks with me. However, there is a difference between having bizarre details and having the entire article be confusing. Maybe it's because I haven't read that other Patter Screamers articles that I didn't understand what the hell was going on, but then again, I shouldn't have to read another specific SCP to understand the one I'm currently reading.

Another smaller gripe is the fact that the SCP is described as a computer. I understand the comparison you're trying to make, but it kinda feels out of place to describe it as a computer rather than just say it functions similarly to one. For a bit I thought the SCP was mechanical rather than organic.

Also, not everyone here knows the scientific names of animals and fauna, so adding the common name in brackets after the scientific name would be helpful to the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could easily write "SCP-2528-B is comprised of the species Ailuropoda Melanoleuca (commonly known as giant panda) and its attendant digestive bacteria." Without breaking up the scientific tone. Many articles do it to make sure the reader knows what creature they are talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1: Use of latin names is pretty common on this site. Generally regarded as a way to improve immersion/uphold the sense that these articles are scientific documents

2: I find the prospect of readers looking up the names and realizing that someone has actually written the phrase " discouraging research into the quantum properties of bamboo and pandas" funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a bit annoying to me, that you literally have to look up the species names (or read that spoiler) in order to get what's going on. In a technical guise or not, this is still a piece of writing, and a piece of writing - particularly fiction - shouldn't have to make the reader do extra credit in order to understand its main gist.

Is that standard in SCP articles, to use the Latin names without using the common English names for it? The animal/plant articles I recall have at least clued the reader in to that much.

I'm a lurker who reads frequently but has never posted, so if I'm going against the grain here, feel free to point out as much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's pretty much spot on.

As for the purpleness: I don't exactly disagree. In part I wanted to keep at least a little of the fever dream aspect that inspired this; mostly it seemed the best way to cram all the references I wanted in there without it being too painfully obvious. It is the weakest part of it, imo. I couldn't find a better way to do it.

Elaboration
– hide block

I can see two main approaches to conveying an alien mind through dialog, I call the Kosh and the Kosh2 methods:
Kosh2: Simple, direct statements. Yes. No. Irrelevant. Go here. Do this.
Alien is seeking simplest common reference frame. Communication is objective oriented. Alternately, alien has a machinelike mind.

Kosh: Metaphor and purple prose.
Alien is trying to find common ground despite have different perceptions. Alternately, alien has fey mind. Or is just dicking with you.

-C wants something, yes, but their mind is more on the fey side of the scale. They don't fully understand themselves what they are talking about. Purple seemed the way to go.

One of my original ideas, which can be seen in my sandbox, has a much more personable -C-1 with a more histrionic -D providing the purple bits, with -C-1 explaining as best it could. That ended up being too blunt.

For reference
– hide block

This thing came about when I tried to mount my headcanon on a fever dream I had.
Dream involved a man berating a panda for eating bamboo when they have a carnivorous digestive track, to which the panda replies "So that we endure". This repeats every thousand years until the heat death of the universe.

The headcanon involves qntm's 001 proposal, 2040, the Pattern Screamers and a bunch of other stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My understanding of what's going on here:

+ Spoilers
- Spoilers

I don't think they're 'literally' computers, so much as the fact that their current structure easily lends itself as a comparison to one.

Essentially, they're not so much an entity from the previous universe so much as the leftovers of an entity from the previous universe. Basically the prior entities embody concepts and bits that simply can't exist in the current one. Most of the prior entities reduced themselves to some very core concepts that could exist, but in doing so lost all their prior knowledge and identities, but at the core retained the essence of themselves. (It's suggested that they're now 'us' in the sense of stuff that's alive on the planet)

But the ones here were too prideful or fearful to sacrifice that much of themselves and tried to fit themselves in via a loophole. Exceepppt, they didn't to a great job and in fitting through said loophole, great chunks of 'self' were annihilated to fit. It's also why they're so weird and basically a kludged together bunch of identities that exist in the interaction between Pandas and Bamboo, and why they're so maimed. But it's also why they remember the previous universe.

Note, I do think the prose is too purple and somewhat impenetrable - but that's my understanding of what's going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't understand the logic in thinking they are computers. Their structure may lend themselves to be similar to a network, but does it really mean they are?

It also might be that the way these things communicate, but I never know what they're saying. Purpleish, dense, vague dialog is a bit tired and usually can be said a lot better a lot simpler. Sure, you might have an idea what's being said here. But for all I can understand and make out, all dialog from 2528-C is gibberish. So what's supoosed to be a final wham note… means nothing to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed a typo - "setp" instead of step

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spoiler
Warned ya

-A is bamboo. All of it. It acts as hard drive/network.
-B are pandas. All of them. They act as cpus/robot arms.
A+B = Computer network.
-C are AIs living in A+B. -C are also relics of a previous incarnation of the universe, and they are terrified of dying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to hesitantly say that after two readthroughs, I have no idea what the fuck is going on. I understand individual subdesignations, I guess, but I do not understand the whole, and it is extremely confusing. How do I start to understand what's going on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot more than the one you showed on the forum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh! Pattern screamers. Haven't seen/heard/read this concept in quite some time.
